availability.p: Published under a Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 7 [Bnf 12926 Transcription]
div.p: Chapter 7

That infidels are not of the forum of the Church is proven secondly from Paul in the first letter to the Corinthians, chapter 5. For what have I (he says) to do with judging those who are outside? Do you not judge those who are inside? For God will judge those who are outside. These words of Paul all doctors, both sacred and not holy, both Greek and Latin, interpret to signify that the Church cannot regularly judge infidels who have never received the Christian faith. The reason for this is. thus now in the Council of Trent, session 14, 2

Because God, who is Lord of all, did not wish His Church to judge these: but reserved judgment to Himself. The interlinear gloss explaining those words those who are outside says outside the Church, He has entrusted to us that we should deal gently with them. Jerome And Jerome there: Do you not judge only those who are inside the Church. Thus he says.

Anselm Again Anselm: Concerning infidels (he says) who are outside, that is, outside the Church, so that I should judge them as ones for you to avoid, as you thought: for we must deal gently with them, so that through love and sweetness they may be won for Christ. And below: For God has not entrusted to you the judging of those who are outside: but He Himself will judge them in the future according to the distinction of His justice, rendering worthy punishments. Thus Anselm.

Athanasius And Athanasius in the same place. Indeed, I address none now of those who are outsiders: for that would exceed my laws. Therefore it would be superfluous for me to enjoin Christ's precepts on those who wander outside Christ's court. For whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law. Thus he says. Augustine The same is taught by Augustine in many places. Theophylact and Bede, Again Nicholas of Gorran interprets the Apostle's words in this way. As if he were to say. To judge infidels does not pertain to me: but rather we must deal gently with them, so that they may be converted. Thus also the Lord willed Luke 12. O man, who made me a judge or divider over you. He was not one of His disciples. Thus he says.

Saint Thomas Again Saint Thomas expounds that passage thus. For what have I to do, that is, what does it pertain to me to judge, that is, to pronounce a sentence of condemnation concerning those who are outside, that is, concerning infidels who are completely outside the Church. For the prelates of the Churches receive spiritual power only over those who have submitted themselves to the faith, according to that passage 2 Corinthians 10: being ready to punish all disobedience when your obedience is complete. Thus Saint Thomas. Moreover, in the treatise against those who impugn religion, chapter 15, to the 4th objection he expounds those words in the same way.

Bruno The same is the opinion of Saint Bruno on the Epistles of Paul, and Richard Richard of Middleton in the 4th book of the Sentences, distinction 7, article 2, question 2 teaching that to the vicar of Christ has not been granted any direct power over those who have not received the sacrament of baptism: which is the door by which one enters the Church militant: whence the Apostle in the first letter to the Corinthians, chapter 5 says what have I to do with those and so on. Whence the gloss says that is, what does it pertain to me to judge. As if he were to say, it does not pertain to me to judge in these matters: but concerning those who are inside the Church by profession and name: do you not judge? Indeed, and God alone will judge those who are outside: He has not entrusted us to judge them now and so on. All this from Richard.

Similarly Saint Thomas in the Summa, 2a 2ae, question 12, article 2 says: It does not pertain to the Church to punish infidelity in those who have never received the faith, according to the Apostle in 1 Corinthians 5: What have I to do with those and so on: But the Church can punish by sentence the infidelity of those who have received the faith. Thus Saint Thomas. If it does not pertain to the Church to punish the infidelity of infidels who have not received the faith: therefore they are not of the forum of the Church.
To the same effect Nicholas of Lyra: expounds the same words in this way: it does not pertain to me because they are outside the forum of the Church.
Therefore all doctors teach from those words of Paul that infidels cannot be punished by the Church because they are not of the forum of the Church.

Moreover, thirdly, that the Church cannot punish infidels is proven by this argument: which I and all that is mine submit to its correction.
It does not pertain to the Church to forcibly remove the worship of idols among those not subject to it
It does not pertain to the Church to forcibly remove the worship of idols: nor to chastise idolaters, at least those not subject to it, therefore idolaters and infidels are not of the ecclesiastical forum. The consequence is now clear from the preceding reasons and authorities.

But the antecedent as to the first part (namely) that it does not pertain to the Church to forcibly remove the worship of idols. Augustine This is proven from Augustine in the book On the Words of the Lord, sermon 6 on the centurion's boy: who writes these words there: Brothers, it pertains to us to speak to you, it pertains to us to speak to Christians. For what have I to do with judging those who are outside? the Apostle himself says: We address those (namely) pagans differently, as if they were weak. We must deal gently with them so that they may hear the truth: in you the corruption must be cut out. If you ask by what means pagans may be conquered, by what means they may be enlightened, by what means they may be called to salvation: abandon all their solemnities: abandon their follies, and if they do not consent to our truth: let them be ashamed of their fewness.

And below: Do not believe their words nor fear them. They call us enemies of their idols. So may God grant and give all things into power as He gave what has been done, for we say this to your charity, that you should not do these things: when it is not in your power to do that. It is the way of wicked men, of madmen: of circumcellions: both where they do not have power to rage. And they hasten to die without cause. Did you hear what we read to you? You who were recently absent from the mapalia? When the land shall have been given to you in power. Deuteronomy 7 and 12 First (it says in power) you shall destroy their altars (he says): you shall break down their groves, and you shall shatter all their monuments. When you shall have received power, do this: where power has not been given to us, we do not do it: where it has been given we do not omit it. Many pagans have these abominations on their estates: do we approach and break them? For first we act so that we may break the idols in their hearts. When they themselves have been made Christians: they either invite us to such a good work: or they anticipate us: now we must pray for them: not be angry at them.

And below: Because therefore God has willed: they think we seek idols everywhere: which when we find in all places we break them. Why? Are there not places before us in which they are? Or do we truly not know where they are? And yet we do not do it: because God has not given it into power. When does God give into power? When he shall be a Christian, whose property it is. Now He willed it to be done by the one whose it is. If he were unwilling to give the place itself to the Church and only command concerning his own property: I do not think the idols should be removed: because it ought to be done with the greatest devotion by Christians: the absent one would be helped. The Christian soul who wishes to give thanks to God from the land: does not want anything to be there to the insult of God. It is too little for them that we do not remove those things from their estates: we do not break them; and they want them to be preserved in ours. We preach against idols: we remove them from hearts: we are persecutors of idols: we profess it. Are we their preservers? I do not do it where I cannot: I do not do it where the owner of the property will complain, but where he wishes it to be done and gives thanks: I shall be guilty, if I do not act. All these things are from Augustine.

From these words it is manifestly elicited that Augustine held that the Church does not regularly have jurisdiction over idolaters: and those subject to itself or its members, nor can it forcibly compel them to abandon the worship of idols: nor shatter their vain images against their will. Where he says: Are there not places before us in which they are? Or do we truly not know where they are? as if he were to say: We do not not know, but we do not do it: because God has not given it to us in power: he manifestly shows that this power has been denied to the Church not as a matter of fact but of law. Since Christians could remove idols by force, as Augustine suggests there. And thus he wishes to teach that the Church in this matter, in removing idols against the will of idolaters, completely lacks power, and thus it is illicit. This opinion of Augustine not only refutes the vain opinion of our adversaries: but also proves false the opinion of Hostiensis in the chapter "quod super his": concerning vows about which I have discussed more fully elsewhere.

But it should be noted that Augustine says that to shatter idols and forbid their worship before we have the pagans in power. Which should be understood as before they are of the forum of the Church through baptism: is the work of demented and mad men, of circumcellions. These circumcellions, according to Isidore in the Etymologies, book 8, chapter 5 were certain heretics who killed themselves, so that they might be held as martyrs.
Circumcellions were called certain heretics who killed themselves so that they might be martyrs
Augustine writes that those are similar to these who attempt to remove the worship of idols where they strive to do this, so that being killed they may be thought martyrs, and therefore he says: Where they do not have power to rage and to wish to die, they hasten without cause and so on. And therefore, if I am not mistaken, against these the Council of Elvira, chapter 60 had its origin, which says: if anyone breaks idols and is killed there: because it is not written in the gospel, nor is it found ever to have been done by the apostles: it has pleased us not to receive them in the number of martyrs. Note from the words of the council that Christ never broke idols, nor did His apostles, in whose time the whole world was raging in the worship of idols: nor did they order anyone else to break idols by force. For indeed if He had done this or commanded it, certainly it would be read in the Gospel. Which truly, if this were expedient or just, Christ and the apostles would have done it without fear of princes: whom they would have terrified either by miracles or by the word of God.

We read that Ananias and Sapphira were rebuked by the words of the apostle Peter and breathed out their souls: and therefore great fear was struck into the others. Thus it is read in Acts 5: But Ananias hearing these words fell down and expired: and great fear came upon all who heard. From which it is elicited that the apostles did not break idols: not because they were poor and destitute of all defense, but because it was not lawful. For they could destroy a man with a word alone, nor did they cease from fear of death or disgrace to do what was conducive to the evangelical work. For they surpassed in power: all princes, caesars and tyrants: and therefore the Holy Spirit calls them princes of the earth in Psalm 44: You will make them princes over all the earth.

But if we sometimes read that the apostles or martyrs commanded demons, when they were brought to sacrifice to idols: to shatter those images of vain gods: that was with the consent of the pagans. Of this kind is what we read in the life of Saint Bartholomew, if you wish all the sick to receive health: take down this idol and break it, and I will consecrate this temple to Christ: which when they tried at the king's command, and could not: the apostle said to the demon: Come out of the image, and break it. These things are in the legend and life of him.

Sometimes they broke idols by divine power: so that by displaying miracles, faith might be given to the evangelical truth which they were announcing. Thus we read in Acts 17 that the apostle Paul went around all the images of idols in Athens, and finally looked upon an altar consecrated to the unknown god, and conversing with Dionysius, first by reasoning and by performing a miracle, drew him to contempt of idols: but not by seizing or overthrowing the idols, to the worship of the true God, so that both he and many others broke the idols with their own hands: which happens to us very often among the Indians.

This indeed is the truly Christian method, that God may first hand over to us in possession the land: that is: that the idolaters may be converted whose idols they are: and consequently of shattering idols and abrogating the worship of idols: namely to teach the worshippers of idols the evangelical truth and to lead them by reasoning to the net of the Church. For then we will lawfully break them (as Augustine said) indeed they themselves, adoring the evangelical light: will break the impious images of demons, and will trample them underfoot with us.

Therefore the Church regularly cannot forcibly break idols: before the pagans have heard and willingly received the evangelical truth. For it is fatal to the expansion of true religion. Unless perhaps the pagans were already very inclined to receive our religion: or if they had voluntarily submitted themselves to our rule: then the worship of idols can be forbidden, with some light laws added. If only there be no scandal at all. Which however will rarely happen: and not unless where the present occasion of things shows that the matter will turn to the advantage of the people. For it is not expedient for the worship of idols to be removed by violence of men: but by the word of God.

For what shall we accomplish: if, with idols forcibly broken, the pagans still ignorant of Christ: secretly offer their sacrifices to their gods in forests, mountains and deserts: where those who oppress them are especially accustomed to find Christ? For perhaps the idols will be removed from temples: but not from hearts. Wherefore Saint Gregory says most excellently: Gregory In vain do we either completely remove ceremonies from infidels or set rules for how they ought to be observed: if through this we cannot gain them? 45th distinction, chapter "qui sincera". Therefore first, Augustine says, let us break the idols in their hearts. But idolatry will be abolished late or never without the preaching of the Gospel and knowledge of the true God, according to Chrysostom on 2 Corinthians, homily 7 and William of Paris in the book on laws.

Hence Saint Thomas in the Summa, 2a 2ae, question 10, article 11 teaches that the rites of pagans ought to be tolerated by the Church: so that hearing the evangelical preaching they may be converted to the faith. He speaks of pagan rites: which are performed in the lands of the Church. For concerning others it was not necessary for him to say they ought to be tolerated. Since nothing pertains to the Church concerning such things except through opportune preaching of the Gospel to persuade them to abandon them. The words of Saint Thomas are these. For the sake of avoiding some evil (he says) (namely) to avoid scandal or dissension which could arise from this: or the impediment to their salvation: who being thus tolerated will little by little be converted to the faith: for on this account indeed even the rites of heretics and pagans the Church sometimes tolerated: when there was a great multitude of infidels. Thus Saint Thomas.

Indeed these things are so manifest: that I think they would satisfy Sepulveda however pertinacious an asserter of his opinion. For what now can he calumniate? He objects to us Saint Thomas against himself. Certainly that holy man wishes pagans to be tolerated and allured to the faith: not violently compelled: to teach is the part of Christians, to compel is the part of tyrants. For what can be assigned as the general reason of all the aforesaid. Since indeed nature itself teaches that the whole human race ought to worship God: but divine worship consists in ceremonies: hence it happens that just as men cannot live without God truly or falsely held for true: so neither without the exercise of any ceremonies. Especially since among gentiles the opinion flourished that by ceremonies and sacrifices: the whole state of the republic was preserved in felicity. Whence if we were to completely remove their ceremonies against their will, besides other many inconveniences which would follow: they would hold the Catholic faith and Christian religion as fleshless, and we would seem to compel them indirectly to receive the faith itself (which is forbidden).

When therefore was there ever such a multitude of infidels in the whole world: that there was just cause for tolerating their rites even if they had been subject to the Church? Or in which other nations could idols more easily be broken in hearts: and thus idolatry be totally abolished altogether as in the western regions of the Indies Most serious scandals be avoided: also the perdition of innumerable souls: if they were taught little by little and tolerated, than among our nations of Indians? Indeed the holy Church itself knows better that it pertains to it in no way to forcibly remove idols and idolatry from infidels of whom we are speaking: but only by the divine word and sweet urgency of reason: in the way it has always been known to have used from its infancy, and will use (as I hope) until the coming of the just judge, its spouse Jesus Christ. Therefore they err and act against the custom of the Church and the doctrine and examples of the holy fathers: doing the contrary or asserting that the contrary ought to be done. And thus finally it is clear that infidels insofar as such are not of the forum or jurisdiction of the Church. See below in chapter 48 more fully.
